title: Process Management & Signals
description: Processes are the fundamental unit of execution on Linux. Every program, daemon, and shell command runs as one or more processes. At Enterprise, understanding process lifecycle, signals, and the /proc filesystem is essential for debugging production issues, building resilient services, and optimizing performance.

sections.title: Process Lifecycle & States
sections.description: A Linux process transitions through states: Running (R), Sleeping (S), Uninterruptible Sleep (D), Zombie (Z), and Stopped (T). The kernel's scheduler allocates CPU time slices (time slices are ~100ms on desktop kernels, configurable via sched_latency_ns). At Enterprise, 'D' state processes blocking I/O are a common cause of system stalls — you diagnose this via ps and /proc//status.
sections.codeBlock:
# === PS (Process Snapshot) ===
ps aux                        # All processes, BSD-style
ps -ef                         # All processes, standard format
ps auxf                        # Forest view (tree with parent-child)
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10  # Top 10 by memory
ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -10  # Top 10 by CPU
ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd,%mem,%cpu --sort=-%cpu  # Custom columns

# === TOP (Real-time) ===
top                            # Interactive
  # Inside top:
  #  1 — toggle per-CPU view
  #  M — sort by memory
  #  P — sort by CPU
  #  k — kill a process
  #  q — quit

htop                           # Better top (color, scroll, tree mode)

# === /proc — The Virtual Filesystem ===
cat /proc/cpuinfo              # CPU details (model, cores, flags)
cat /proc/meminfo              # Memory details (total, free, cached)
cat /proc/loadavg              # Load averages + running/total processes
cat /proc/1/cmdline            # Command line of PID 1 (init/systemd)
ls /proc/<PID>/fd/             # Open file descriptors (great for leaking FDs)
cat /proc/<PID>/status         # Process state, memory, capabilities
cat /proc/<PID>/maps           # Memory-mapped regions
cat /proc/<PID>/environ        # Environment variables (null-separated, use tr)
tr '\0' '\n' < /proc/<PID>/environ

# === Key fields in ps ===
# PID   — Process ID
# PPID  — Parent Process ID
# %CPU  — CPU usage (% of one core)
# %MEM  — Memory usage (% of total RAM)
# VSZ   — Virtual memory size
# RSS   — Resident Set Size (actual physical memory)
# STAT  — Process state (R, S, D, Z, T, etc.)
# WCHAN — Kernel function the process is sleeping in

# === Finding specific processes ===
pgrep -f "python server.py"     # Find PID by pattern
pgrep -u www-data              # All processes for a user
pidof nginx                     # Find PIDs of named process
pkill -f "stale_script"        # Kill processes matching pattern

sections.title: Signals: The Linux Interrupt System
sections.description: Signals are asynchronous notifications sent to processes. They can be generated by the kernel (SIGSEGV on segfault), by user actions (Ctrl+C = SIGINT), or by other processes (kill command). Understanding signals is critical for writing robust daemons and debugging crashes in production. At Enterprise, signal handlers must be carefully written to avoid race conditions and deadlocks.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Common Signals ===
# SIGTERM (15) — Graceful termination (default for kill)
# SIGKILL (9)  — Force kill (cannot be caught/ignored)
# SIGINT  (2)  — Interrupt from keyboard (Ctrl+C)
# SIGQUIT (3)  — Quit with core dump (Ctrl+\)
# SIGHUP  (1)  — Hangup (reload config for many daemons)
# SIGUSR1/2 (10,12) — User-defined signals
# SIGSTOP (19) — Stop process (cannot be caught/ignored)
# SIGCONT (18) — Continue a stopped process
# SIGSEGV (11) — Segmentation fault (invalid memory access)
# SIGPIPE (13) — Write to broken pipe (common in pipes)

# === Sending signals ===
kill -15 <PID>                 # SIGTERM (graceful)
kill -9 <PID>                  # SIGKILL (force, use as last resort)
kill -1 <PID>                  # SIGHUP (reload config)
kill -SIGUSR1 <PID>            # Send user-defined signal
killall nginx                   # Send signal to all matching processes

# === Trapping signals in scripts ===
#!/bin/bash
cleanup() {
    echo "Cleaning up..."
    rm -f /tmp/lockfile
    exit 0
}
trap cleanup SIGTERM SIGHUP SIGINT

# Run indefinitely until killed
echo "PID: $$"; while true; do sleep 1; done

# === Zombie Processes ===
# A zombie process (STAT=Z) has exited but its parent hasn't called wait().
# Zombies are harmless but indicate a buggy parent. They consume a PID + entry.
# You cannot kill a zombie. You must kill its parent (PPID) to let init reap it.

# === Process Priority & Niceness ===
# Nice values: -20 (highest priority) to +19 (lowest priority)
nice -n 10 ./slow_task           # Run with lower priority (nice=10)
renice -n -5 -p <PID>           # Change priority of running process
chrt -r 1 ./realtime_task       # Set real-time scheduling (SCHED_RR)

# === Limits ===
ulimit -a                        # All limits for current shell
ulimit -n 65536                  # Max open file descriptors
cat /proc/<PID>/limits           # Limits for a specific process

sections.title: Daemons & Process Supervision
sections.description: A daemon is a background process that runs independently of any terminal. Modern Linux uses systemd for service supervision, but legacy approaches include init.d scripts and supervisord. In Enterprise environments, every service has a supervision system that auto-restarts on failure and collects metrics.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Systemd Service ===
systemctl status nginx           # Check status
systemctl start nginx            # Start service
systemctl stop nginx             # Stop service
systemctl restart nginx          # Restart
systemctl enable nginx           # Start on boot
systemctl disable nginx          # Remove from autostart
systemctl daemon-reload          # Reload unit files after editing
journalctl -u nginx              # View logs for service

# === Background & Daemonize ===
# Simple backgrounding
./server.sh &

# Using nohup to survive terminal close
nohup ./long_task > output.log 2>&1 &

disown                           # Remove from shell job table

# daemon(3) / double-fork — traditional daemon creation
# 1. Fork -> parent exits -> child orphaned to init
# 2. Child calls setsid() to create new session
# 3. Fork again -> second child continues
# 4. Close stdin/stdout/stderr, chdir to /
# This is handled automatically by systemd unit files.

# === Enterprise pattern: Health checks ===
# Every daemon should answer a health check endpoint.
# Pattern: GET /healthz returns 200 OK
kill -0 <PID>                    # Check if process exists (no signal sent)
if kill -0 "$PID" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Process $PID is running"
fi

sections.title: cgroups & Containers (Modern Process Isolation)
sections.description: Control groups (cgroups) limit and account for resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O) across process hierarchies. Docker and Kubernetes are built on cgroups + namespaces. At Enterprise, every production process runs inside a cgroup — resource limits are non-negotiable.
sections.codeBlock:
# === cgroups v2 (modern) ===
# Hierarchy at /sys/fs/cgroup/
ls /sys/fs/cgroup/

# Create a cgroup and set limits
sudo mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/myapp
sudo sh -c 'echo "100000 200000" > /sys/fs/cgroup/myapp/cpu.max'
sudo sh -c 'echo "100M" > /sys/fs/cgroup/myapp/memory.max'

# Add a process to the cgroup
echo $PID | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/myapp/cgroup.procs

# === cgroups v1 (legacy) ===
# CPU shares (relative weight, default 1024)
sudo cgcreate -g cpu:mygroup
sudo cgset -r cpu.shares=512 mygroup
sudo cgclassify -g cpu:mygroup <PID>

# === Inspect container cgroups ===
# Inside a Docker container:
cat /proc/1/cgroup

# From host:
ls /sys/fs/cgroup/system.slice/docker-<CONTAINER_ID>.scope/

# === Resource limits via systemd ===
# In a systemd unit file:
[Service]
MemoryMax=256M
CPUQuota=50%
TasksMax=100

# === /proc/self/status !== cgroup limits
# Remember: /proc/meminfo shows host memory, not cgroup memory.
# Use cgroup memory stats for container-aware monitoring.
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.current

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'A process on a production server is stuck in 'D' (uninterruptible sleep) state. What does this mean, and how do you diagnose it?'

A: 'D state means the process is waiting on I/O (disk or network) in a kernel operation that cannot be interrupted. Common causes: NFS server timeout, disk controller failure, slow storage. Diagnose by checking ps aux | grep ' D' to find the PID, then look at /proc//stack to see which kernel function is blocking, and dmesg | tail for I/O errors. If the underlying device recovers, processes leave D state — but sometimes they're stuck permanently and need a reboot.'

Q: 'Explain the difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL. When would you use each?'

A: 'SIGTERM (15) is graceful termination — the process can catch it, clean up resources, flush buffers, and exit cleanly. SIGKILL (9) cannot be caught or ignored — the kernel immediately terminates the process without cleanup. Always try SIGTERM first. Use SIGKILL only when a process is ignoring SIGTERM or is stuck in an unkillable state (like D state, where even SIGKILL is queued and won't take effect until the kernel operation completes). At Enterprise, our service managers (like our internal equivalent of systemd) send SIGTERM and wait 30 seconds before escalating to SIGKILL.'

Q: 'What is a zombie process, and why can't you kill it?'

A: 'A zombie process has already exited but still has an entry in the process table because its parent hasn't called wait() / waitpid() to read its exit status. It's dead — 'kill' won't work because there's nothing to signal. The fix is to kill the parent (PPID) so init (PID 1) adopts and reaps the zombie children. In production, a long-running parent with a buggy signal handler can accumulate thousands of zombies, exhausting the PID table.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• signal(7) man page — Complete Linux signal reference
  • cgroups v2 Documentation — Kernel.org official docs
  • The Linux Programming Interface — Michael Kerrisk, the definitive reference on Linux processes and signals

learningObjectives: Understand signals: The Linux Interrupt System

analogy.title: Processes as Kitchen Staff
analogy.description: Each process is a chef. The kernel is the head chef assigning tasks (scheduling). Fork() creates a duplicate chef. Exec() gives the chef a new recipe. Signals are kitchen bells - SIGTERM is "finish your dish," SIGKILL is "leave immediately." Zombies are chefs who finished but nobody collected their timesheet.

commonMistakes.solution: Send SIGTERM first (kill PID); wait for graceful shutdown; only use SIGKILL if process ignores SIGTERM

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Process Lifecycle & States
practiceLabs.task: Run `ps` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ps` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Signals: The Linux Interrupt System
practiceLabs.task: Run `top` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man top` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Daemons & Process Supervision
practiceLabs.task: Run `htop` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man htop` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ps` | Process Lifecycle & States |
| `top` | Process Lifecycle & States |
| `htop` | Process Lifecycle & States |
| `kill` | Signals: The Linux Interrupt System |
| `killall` | Signals: The Linux Interrupt System |
| `cleanup` | Signals: The Linux Interrupt System |
| `systemctl` | Daemons & Process Supervision |
| `journalctl` | Daemons & Process Supervision |
| `nohup` | Daemons & Process Supervision |
| `ls` | cgroups & Containers (Modern Process Isolation) |
| `sudo` | cgroups & Containers (Modern Process Isolation) |
| `echo` | cgroups & Containers (Modern Process Isolation) |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ps`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: processes operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Process Lifecycle & States?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Process Lifecycle & States is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ps` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the processes directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a processes directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a processes scenario, your monitoring alerts show ps failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  At FAANG scale, you rarely SSH into individual boxes to check processes
FAANG incident root cause: a process inherited a low ulimit from its parent and hit 'too many open files' during traffic spike
productionTips: production incident where auto-scaling has failed — your ability to navigate /proc, interpret process states, and trace system calls is what separates the engineer who fixes the issue in 5 minutes from the one who's still reading man pages 30 minutes later
Production Tip:  Set ulimit -n (file descriptors) appropriately for your workload
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, process management & signals is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
